serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tomato beef brisket noodle is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of wheat noodles in tomato-beef broth topped with braised beef brisket pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup with tomato — composite dish estimation based on USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef brisket (braised), Wheat noodles (cooked), Tomatoes (raw/cooked), and Beef broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tomato beef brisket noodle as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted-ingredient model using USDA FoodData Central data for: braised beef brisket (~25% by weight, FDC ID 174032), cooked wheat noodles (~40% by weight, FDC ID 169730), tomato-based broth (~30% by weight, FDC ID 170457 for tomatoes and FDC ID 172956 for beef broth), and cooking oil/seasonings (~5%). Per-100g values are derived from this composite model. HPB Singapore Foodo was checked; no exact match for this dish was found. FAO/INFOODS SEA was also checked; no exact match found.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat-based, cooked — GI analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tomato beef brisket noodle as a composite dish. GI of 52 is estimated based on the dominant glycemic component: cooked wheat noodles (GI ~52, low GI, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). The beef brisket and tomato broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 45.9) / 100 ≈ 23.9, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 10.2 × 4.5 = 45.9g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and soy sauce (~320 mg per 100g; ~1440 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single bowl can contribute approximately 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~23.9 per serving) driven by wheat noodle carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the moderate glycemic load per serving; choosing a smaller noodle portion, requesting lower-GI noodle alternatives (e.g. glass noodles), and pairing with the protein-rich beef brisket can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef brisket (~1.4g per 100g) and high sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency due to the combined saturated fat and sodium load; opting for leaner cuts or smaller brisket portions and requesting less broth can reduce cardiovascular risk exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Beef Brisket Noodle (番茄牛腩面 / 番茄牛腩麵) is a popular Chinese noodle dish consisting of wheat noodles served in a rich, tangy tomato-based beef broth, topped with tender braised beef brisket. The dish originates from Cantonese cuisine and is widely served across Hong Kong, mainland China, and Singapore. The beef brisket is slow-braised with tomatoes, soy sauce, ginger, garlic, star anise, and sugar until deeply flavourful and fork-tender. The tomato broth is simultaneously savoury and slightly acidic, providing a bright contrast to the richness of the braised meat. The noodles are typically smooth wheat noodles or flat rice noodles, absorbing the broth's umami depth. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~7.2g per 100g) from the beef brisket, moderate carbohydrates (~10.2g per 100g) from the noodles, and a notable sodium content (~320mg per 100g) from the broth and soy sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI), driven by the wheat noodle component. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension due to high sodium per serving (~1440mg), and warrants moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to moderate glycemic load (~23.9 per bowl). The beef brisket contributes saturated fat, making portion control relevant for cardiovascular health. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains gluten and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact match for tomato beef brisket noodle was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-ingredient composite model using USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) data for braised beef brisket, cooked wheat noodles, tomatoes, and beef broth. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked wheat noodles, GI 52) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish was identified. Values should be updated if an exact match becomes available in HPB Singapore Foodo or a comparable regional database. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for the component ingredients at the level of precision used in this composite model. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles; actual GI of the composite dish may differ depending on noodle type, cooking time, and broth composition. 4. Sodium content is a significant concern per serving; restaurant preparations may vary considerably. 5. Halal status is listed as false because beef brisket sourcing and preparation method at the specific restaurant (Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant) cannot be confirmed as halal-certified from available data. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the composite ingredient model and carry medium confidence. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for these ingredient components.
